summary: It's been a wild day across the BeltLine corridor and beyond. Reynoldstown finally saw some construction action on that long-empty Chester Avenue corner, while Inman Park welcomed a new Asian-American comfort food spot. Meanwhile, downtown's dealing with major airport chaos as TSA agents call out in droves.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown's Chester Avenue Corner Finally Springs to Life
topStory.summary: After nearly five years of sitting as an empty lot, construction has officially begun on 85 townhomes and condos at Chester and Fulton Terrace. Embry Development took over the project from Toll Brothers and is moving forward with what they're calling Camber Crossing. The site is just a block from the BeltLine's Eastside Trail, so expect these units to go fast once they hit the market.
topStory.body:
Remember that corner lot at Chester and Fulton Terrace that's been sitting empty since 2021? Well, grab your hard hats because construction is finally happening. Embry Development has broken ground on what they're calling Camber Crossing, bringing 85 residential units to that prime spot just a block west of the BeltLine's Eastside Trail.

The Atlanta-based developer picked up the project from Toll Brothers two years ago, and frankly, it's about time. This corner has been crying out for development ever since the original buildings were cleared. The mix of townhomes and for-sale condos should fill a real need in Reynoldstown, especially with the neighborhood's continued growth along the BeltLine corridor.

With Memorial Drive right there and easy BeltLine access, these units are going to be hot commodities. Embry Development managing partners confirm they're moving full steam ahead after years of planning and permitting delays. It's one more sign that the eastside corridor continues to evolve, even when projects take longer than anyone would like.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction activity at the Camber Crossing development site in Reynoldstown

stories.imageAlt: Construction activity at the Camber Crossing development site in Reynoldstown

stories.headline: Decatur's Missing Middle Housing Gets Real at Cottages at Midway
stories.summary: This village-style community of 18 standalone houses is about to soft-launch on Midway Road, offering a rare chance at relatively affordable single-family housing near Decatur. With median home values in Decatur hitting over $700k, this project aims to fill the gap with houses built around shared courtyards and greenspace. It's the kind of thoughtful infill development we need more of.
stories.body:
Finally, someone's doing missing middle housing right in Decatur. Cottages at Midway is getting ready for its soft launch next week, bringing 18 for-sale houses to a pair of underused lots on Midway Road. The genius here is the village concept - instead of cramming as many units as possible onto the 2.1 acres, they're creating a community around shared courtyards and greenspace.

With Decatur home prices pushing past $700,000, this mixed-income project is trying to tackle the affordability crisis that's pricing out teachers, firefighters, and young families. The standalone houses offer that single-family feel everyone wants, but in a more sustainable, community-oriented package. Plus, you're right near major commercial corridors and downtown Decatur.

This is exactly the kind of thoughtful infill development we need to see more of across metro Atlanta. Instead of sprawling further out, projects like this show how we can add housing density while preserving neighborhood character. The developers are calling it a pocket neighborhood, and honestly, that's a perfect description of what good urban planning looks like.
stories.imageAlt: Site map showing the layout of Cottages at Midway development in Decatur

stories.headline: West End BeltLine Townhomes Taking Shape at GW Parkside
stories.summary: The first seven units of what used to be called Hopkins and White Townhomes are nearing completion along the BeltLine's westside trail. Developer Ackerman & Co. (yes, the Lee + White folks) renamed it GW Parkside and plans to bring 23 for-sale townhomes to market this spring. It's rare to find new for-sale townhomes along the BeltLine's southwest stretch.
stories.body:
Down on Hopkins Street near the BeltLine's westside trail, something exciting is taking shape. Ackerman & Co. has been quietly working on what they're now calling GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes), and the first seven units are looking good. These are the same developers behind Lee + White, so they know how to do the BeltLine right.

What makes this project special is that it's bringing for-sale townhomes to a stretch of the BeltLine that doesn't see much new residential construction. The West End has been waiting for this kind of development, and 23 new townhomes should help meet demand from people who want to live near the trail without paying Inman Park or Old Fourth Ward prices.

The timing feels right too. Lee + White has proven there's appetite for mixed-use development in this part of town, and now GW Parkside can capture some of that energy in a residential format. With onsite signage promising spring availability, these units should start hitting the market just as BeltLine season kicks into high gear.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes development in West End

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Soft Shell Crab Bao to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez just opened their first restaurant on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. This Asian-American comfort food spot serves everything from chicken longanisa bao to Thai tea martinis, with Peking chicken platters that feed two for just $25. It's the kind of approachable, creative cooking that makes a neighborhood restaurant special.
stories.body:
You know that spot on Elizabeth Street just off the Freedom Park Trail? Well, it's got new life as Mushi Ni, the first restaurant from chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez. These two have serious credentials (Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in NYC, Le's got deep Atlanta roots), and their pop-up-turned-restaurant concept is exactly what Inman Park needed.

The menu hits all the right notes for casual dining with a twist. Soft shell crab bao might sound fancy, but it's served alongside umami fries and crab Rangoon puffs that make this place feel approachable rather than precious. The Peking chicken platter feeding two people for $25 is going to be a neighborhood favorite, especially when paired with one of their cocktails like that Thai tea martini.

What I love about this opening is how it fits into Inman Park's dining scene. You've got your upscale spots and your casual hangouts, but Mushi Ni occupies that sweet spot in between. Plus, being right off the Freedom Park Trail means you can grab dinner after a walk or bike ride. Smart location, solid concept, and the kind of chef-driven food that builds a following.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken dish at Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue's Smoking Room Has the Best View in Atlanta
stories.summary: John Lewis just opened his third location at Ansley Mall, and the smokehouse setup is unlike anything you've seen. Six 22-foot smokers are housed in a loft with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. The brisket is already some of the best in Atlanta, and that's saying something in this town.
stories.body:
I've been to a lot of barbecue joints, but I've never seen a smokehouse like the one at Lewis Barbecue's new Ansley Mall location. Most places stick their smokers in back rooms or outbuildings, but John Lewis put his six 22-foot smokers in a loft with floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking the BeltLine. It looks like the fanciest engine room in Georgia, and it's producing some serious barbecue.

Lewis helped weld these smokers himself out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks, and they run 24/7 in staggered eight-hour shifts. That factory-like precision is paying off in the final product. The brisket is already competing with Atlanta's best, which is no small feat in a town that takes its barbecue seriously. Plus, you're literally steps from the BeltLine via that new pedestrian bridge.

What sets this apart from Lewis's Austin and Charleston locations is the Atlanta-specific touches. The location behind The Cook's Warehouse makes perfect sense for the food-obsessed crowd, and having those smokers visible to BeltLine traffic is brilliant marketing. This isn't just another barbecue spot, it's a destination that happens to serve incredible food.
stories.imageAlt: Barbecue platter at Lewis Barbecue showing brisket and sides

stories.headline: Decatur's West Howard Gets Its Complete Streets Makeover
stories.summary: After nearly a decade of planning, the West Howard Avenue transformation from drag strip to people-friendly street is almost done. The one-mile stretch now features two-way bike lanes with concrete barriers, upgraded sidewalks, and traffic calming measures from Commerce Drive to Paden Circle. It's a textbook example of how to retrofit car-centric infrastructure for everyone.
stories.body:
West Howard Avenue used to be a high-speed nightmare for anyone not in a car, but that's changing fast. The Complete Streets project that's been nearly a decade in the making is finally wrapping up, and the transformation is impressive. We're talking about a full mile of road that now includes protected two-way bike lanes, wider sidewalks, and traffic calming features that actually make you want to walk or bike.

The project runs from Commerce Drive near Decatur High all the way to Paden Circle at East Lake MARTA, which means it connects some serious destinations. Those concrete barriers protecting the bike lanes aren't just paint on pavement - they're real infrastructure that makes cycling feel safe. Combined with the sidewalk improvements and new crosswalks, this is how you retrofit suburban-style roads for urban living.

This kind of infrastructure investment is exactly what Decatur needs as it continues to densify. Instead of just accommodating more cars, they're creating options for people to get around without driving. The timing is perfect too, with East Lake MARTA right there and downtown Decatur within easy biking distance. Other cities should be taking notes.
stories.imageAlt: New protected bike lanes and sidewalks on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: Seven-Year-Old Dies in Accidental Shooting in DeKalb County
stories.summary: A tragic accident on Loveless Drive in Ellenwood took the life of a 7-year-old boy who family members say got hold of a gun and shot himself. DeKalb County police are investigating the circumstances, but this heartbreaking incident highlights the ongoing issue of unsecured firearms around children. The family and neighborhood are devastated.
stories.body:
This is the kind of story that breaks your heart and makes you furious at the same time. A 7-year-old boy died early Wednesday morning on Loveless Drive in Ellenwood after what family members are calling an accidental shooting. According to relatives at the scene, the child got hold of a gun and it discharged while he was playing with it.

DeKalb County police are treating this as an unintended discharge of a weapon, which is their way of saying this didn't need to happen. The investigation is ongoing, but the basic facts are devastating: another young life lost because a firearm wasn't properly secured. The road was blocked for hours as investigators worked the scene and grieving family and friends gathered.

These kinds of preventable tragedies happen way too often, and they're a reminder that gun safety isn't just about training - it's about making sure weapons are locked away from children. Safe storage laws exist for a reason, and stories like this show why they matter. A family is destroyed, a community is mourning, and a 7-year-old boy is gone forever.
stories.imageAlt: Police investigation scene on Loveless Drive in DeKalb County

stories.headline: Atlanta Council Advances ICE Detention Restrictions
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously moved forward two resolutions that would ban ICE detention facilities in city limits and require documentation when federal agents are present during police operations. The measures head to the full council on April 20, representing Atlanta's latest pushback against federal immigration enforcement. Dozens of residents packed the meeting to advocate for the restrictions.
stories.body:
Atlanta's taking another stand on immigration enforcement. The Public Safety Committee voted unanimously to advance two resolutions targeting ICE operations within city limits. One would ban ICE detention facilities outright, while the other requires Atlanta police to document when federal immigration agents are present during their operations. Both head to the full council for a vote on April 20.

Dozens of residents showed up to advocate for these measures, and the committee clearly heard them. The documentation requirement is particularly interesting because it creates a paper trail of federal involvement in local policing, which could prove valuable if legal challenges arise down the road. The detention facility ban is more symbolic but sends a clear message about Atlanta's values.

This comes as ICE agents are deployed at Hartsfield-Jackson to help with TSA shortages, which has residents on edge about expanded federal presence. The timing isn't coincidental. Atlanta has consistently positioned itself as a welcoming city, and these resolutions continue that tradition while creating some legal protections for residents and clear guidelines for police interactions with federal agents.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council Public Safety Committee meeting with residents in attendance

stories.headline: Parents Protest DeKalb School Closure Plans at Lakeside
stories.summary: Oak Grove and Evansdale families took to the streets outside Lakeside High during Monday's school closure meeting, demanding a student-first approach to the controversial Student Assignment Project. Parents are frustrated with what they see as lip service from the district rather than genuine community input. The proposed closures have sparked fierce opposition across DeKalb County.
stories.body:
The scene outside Lakeside High on Monday night said everything about how DeKalb parents feel about the school closure process. Oak Grove and Evansdale families brought their kids out with signs demanding small schools and a people-first approach to the controversial Student Assignment Project. This wasn't just another public comment session - it was families fighting for their schools.

Samuel Howe from Oak Grove summed up the frustration perfectly: they're giving parents lip service instead of actually listening. The district keeps calling this a process, but parents see it as a done deal wrapped in the veneer of community input. When hundreds of angry parents pack a meeting, that's not a communication problem - that's a community in revolt.

What's particularly galling is how this affects some of DeKalb's strongest school communities. Oak Grove and Evansdale aren't failing schools that need dramatic intervention. They're neighborhood schools that families chose specifically because they work. The fact that kids were out there protesting alongside their parents shows how deeply this has affected entire communities, not just district budgets.
stories.imageAlt: Parents and children protesting outside Lakeside High School with signs about school closures
